coredata.dc:title: Acoustic shock wave processing on amorphous carbon quantum dots – correlation between spectroscopic-morphological–magnetic and electrical conductivity properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Aswathappa, Sivakumar
Dai, Lidong
Sahaya Jude Dhas, S.
Matheswaran, Priyadharshini
Kumar, Raju Suresh
Thangavel, Vasanthi
Vijayakumar, V.N.
coredata.dc:description: In this context, we report the acoustic shock wave processing on amorphous Carbon quantum dots (a-CQD) such that its degree of graphitization and magnetic properties are analyzed. Analytical techniques such as the Raman spectrometer, high resonation–transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) are utilized to understand the structural, morphological and magnetic properties changes under shocked conditions. The Raman spectroscopic data reveal that the ID/IG ratio is slightly reduced under shocked conditions and the observed values are 0.78, 0.77 and 0.75 for the respective 0, 150 and 300 shocked conditions. The formation of ultra-short-range graphitic nanostructures is confirmed by HR-TEM results at the 300-shocked condition. The most convincing and supporting results for the enhancement of the degree of graphitization are found by the outcome of the magnetic properties such that the saturation magnetization (Ms) is found to be linearly reduced with respect to the number of shock pulses and the observed values are 0.869, 0.757 and 0.710 emu/g for the 0, 150 and 300 shocked conditions, respectively. The electrical resistance of the a-CQD is found to be linearly reduced with respect to the number of shock pulses due to the reduction of sp3 carbon networks. The formation of crystalline graphitic nanostructures in amorphous CQD is explained on the basis of the hot-spot nucleation mechanism. Based on the obtained Raman, HR-TEM and VSM results, during the shocked conditions, slight enhancement of the graphitization is observed; however, from the structural stability point of view, the Carbon dots have high shock resistance than that of the amorphous carbon nanoparticles, multi-wall carbon nanotubes, and reduced graphene oxide nanoparticles. Hence, the title quantum dots can be strongly considered for the applications of device fabrication.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Shock waves
Raman spectroscopy
Saturation magnetization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Acoustic shock-induced transformation of amorphous CQD to graphitic nano structures
Contribution of the amorphous and crystalline graphitic nanostructures in magnetic ordering
Relationship of the magnetic ordering and degree of graphitization
Role of surface chemistry of the a-CQD in electrical conductivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Raman spectra of the control and shocked a-CQD.
Lorentzian peak fitting profiles of the Raman spectra (a) the control (b)150 shocks (d) 300 shocked amorphous CQD (d) ID/IG ratio with respect to the number of shock pulses.
(a) D-band shift (b) G-band shift (c) D-band full width at half maximum values (d) G-band full width at half maximum values against the number of shock pulses.
(a) Pictorial image of the tabletop pressure-driven Reddy tube (b) Schematic diagram of the conversion of the shock wave induced amorphous carbon to graphitic structures.
HR-TEM images of the control and shocked amorphous CQD (a–c) control, (d–f) 150- (g–i) and 300-shocked conditions.
Higher magnification HR-TEM images of the control and shocked amorphous CQD (a–c) control, (d–f) 150 (g–i) 300 shocked conditions.
M–H loops of the control and shocked amorphous CQD.
(a) Zoomed-in portions of the M − H loops of the control and shocked amorphous CQD (b) typical line diagrams of the Zoomed-in portions of the M − H loops of the control and shocked amorphous CQD.
Relation between the density of the graphic nanostructures and saturation magnetization for the control and shocked CQD samples.
Nyquist plots of the control and shocked a-CQD samples.

head.ce:title: Acoustic shock wave processing on amorphous carbon quantum dots – correlation between spectroscopic-morphological–magnetic and electrical conductivity properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this context, we report the acoustic shock wave processing on amorphous Carbon quantum dots (a-CQD) such that its degree of graphitization and magnetic properties are analyzed. Analytical techniques such as the Raman spectrometer, high resonation–transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) are utilized to understand the structural, morphological and magnetic properties changes under shocked conditions. The Raman spectroscopic data reveal that the ID/IG ratio is slightly reduced under shocked conditions and the observed values are 0.78, 0.77 and 0.75 for the respective 0, 150 and 300 shocked conditions. The formation of ultra-short-range graphitic nanostructures is confirmed by HR-TEM results at the 300-shocked condition. The most convincing and supporting results for the enhancement of the degree of graphitization are found by the outcome of the magnetic properties such that the saturation magnetization (Ms) is found to be linearly reduced with respect to the number of shock pulses and the observed values are 0.869, 0.757 and 0.710 emu/g for the 0, 150 and 300 shocked conditions, respectively. The electrical resistance of the a-CQD is found to be linearly reduced with respect to the number of shock pulses due to the reduction of sp3 carbon networks. The formation of crystalline graphitic nanostructures in amorphous CQD is explained on the basis of the hot-spot nucleation mechanism. Based on the obtained Raman, HR-TEM and VSM results, during the shocked conditions, slight enhancement of the graphitization is observed; however, from the structural stability point of view, the Carbon dots have high shock resistance than that of the amorphous carbon nanoparticles, multi-wall carbon nanotubes, and reduced graphene oxide nanoparticles. Hence, the title quantum dots can be strongly considered for the applications of device fabrication.

ce:section.ce:para: The term ‘crystal’ is an ordered periodic array of long-range order and such types of ordered arrangements of crystals and their functional features have been extensively studied by various research groups all across the globe because of their boundless applications in each and every field of science and technology [1–4]. Crystalline materials undergo modifications in degrees of order as and when drastic changes and fluctuations occur in extreme environmental conditions such as static high pressure [5], temperature [6], laser shock waves [7,8], gamma irradiation [9] and acoustic shocked conditions [10,11] such that their characteristic nature tend to be disturbed which necessitates a comprehensive understanding of the probable changes in the atomic arrangements which inurn impose modifications in the functional properties of these materials. Such understating of crystalline materials' structures and their properties with the inclusion of bulk and nanomaterials are highly known at static high-pressure-temperature conditions [12,13], whereas such an understating of the quantum dots' crystal structures and their properties remains to be under the cloud such that much of the work is yet to be accomplished to clear the air. To date, comparatively very few reports have been made on the topic of the pressure compression outcomes on quantum dots [14,15].

In recent years, compared to the bulk and nanocrystals, quantum dots have gained a tremendous amount of research interest because of their flexibility in surface modification, high water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high photo-stability compared to the 1D and 2D dimensional nanomaterials and the best example for quantum dots is carbon quantum dots (CQD) [16,17]. Carbon dots (CDs), a new rising star in the carbon family, have attracted considerable attention due to their excellent and tunable properties. From a crystallographic stability point of view, dots have gained much higher structural stability than the conventional 1D and 2D nanomaterials against the pressure effects. Carbon is one of the universal elements predominantly used for pressure and temperature-assisted experiments and it can exist in different forms: mainly, graphite [18], graphene [19], diamond [20], fullerene [21], tubes [22] and dots [23]. Among the above-mentioned forms, graphite, graphene, fullerene, and crystal structure stability of the tubes as well as their related properties have been extensively studied under ambient conditions. Moreover, under external stimuli such as high pressure and temperature conditions, there have been several interesting results witnessed which include graphite to diamond conversion [24,25]. So far, a variety of synthesis processes of carbon quantum dots and their functional properties have been reported under ambient conditions [26–30] whereas, only a few results have been reported on materials under high-pressure compression conditions [31–33]. Pengtao et al., have demonstrated the solvent-dependent piezochromism of CDs by the DAC experiment and found a red- and blue-shift piezochromism of CDs originating from increased pi–pi stacking under high pressure [31]. Siyu Lu et al. have reported the pressure (0–22.84 GPa) assisted tuning of luminescence properties from yellow (557 nm) to blue-green (491 nm) [32]. Followed by the article, Qi Wang et al., have reported the reversible pressure-induced enhancement of the red emission of carbon dots [33]. Even though few luminescences-related reports have been made to understand the pressure effects in CD, there is no report related to the Raman features and magnetic properties.

Following the static pressure-temperature-induced phase transitions in carbon materials, in recent years, research on dynamic acoustic shock wave effects of carbon materials has emerged as one of the most active research topics and a few publications have been made using simple carbon materials such as graphite, fullerene, tubes, and amorphous carbon particles. Koteeswara Reddy et al. have performed acoustic shock waves-processing on graphitic thin films using a free-piston-driven conventional shock tube with a transient pressure of 6 MPa and found an enhancement in the disordered structure. The ID/IG ratio of the control and shocked particles is obtained to be 1.07 to 1.11 and also found a rapid enhancement of the electrical resistance due to the enhancement in the disordered structure and sp3 components [34]. Biennier et al., have performed the same experiment (with a transient temperature of 3869 K) making use of the starting material of highly crystalline fullerene and found an amorphous structure under shocked conditions [35]. Recently, Arijit Roy et al., have reported the phase transition of amorphous carbon into short-range order carbon onions utilizing the shock transient temperature of 7300 K such that the ID/IG ratio of the control and shocked particles are found to be 1.62 and 1.50 [36]. Following the conventional high enthalpy shock tube experiments, tabletop pressure-driven shock tube experiments have also come up with a few interesting results arising out of the carbon materials. Our research group could demonstrate amorphous multiwall carbon nanotubes tuning into the crystalline state at the 300-shocked condition [37]. Furthermore, similar kinds of amorphous to crystalline phase transitions have been found for the reduced graphene oxide for which the obtained ID/IG ratios are found to be 1.24, 1.17, 1 and 0.927 for 0, 100, 200 and 400 shocks, respectively [38]. Based on the existing literature reports, experiments conducted by employing tabletop pressure-driven shock tubes are capable of producing amorphous to crystalline phase transitions in carbon materials like that of the conventional high enthalpy shock tubes. Moreover, under shocked conditions, the amorphous carbon components (non-sp2) are converted into the crystalline component (sp2) and such high sp2 carbon materials attract a great demand for industrial applications.

In the present work, we have considered the amorphous CQD for the shock wave recovery experiment for the following reasons: The CQD has a high level of technological importance and hence, the investigation of the structural stability under shocked conditions is required. Based on the previous studies, most of the carbon specimens exhibit amorphous to crystalline phase transitions and no precise knowledge could be drawn as to how such a phase transition takes place. This article intends to provide an upgraded knowledge of the conversion of amorphous components into crystalline components under shocked conditions.

The synthesis procedure of the CQD has been described in the previous reports [39]. Briefly, CQDs were prepared from bio-waste (Citrus X sinensis) by employing a hydrothermal method. Locally collected Citrus X sinensis peels (orange peels) were initially washed with water and dried in sunlight such that it was carbonized in a hot air oven at 150 °C for 12 h. The pre-treated Citrus X sinensis peels were washed in dilute sulphuric acid followed by water and then it was filtered and dried at 180 °C for 2 h. To oxidize the obtained orange peel, it was soaked in sodium hypochlorite solution for 5 h and thereafter, it was washed with double distilled water till it reached a pH of 7. After that, the resultant mixture was hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 2 h. The Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was utilized, was then cooled down naturally, and the obtained brown solution was washed with dichloromethane. Finally, the obtained resultant was centrifuged and vacuum dried thereby Carbon dots could be synthesized.

Shock waves with a Mach number of 2.2 and transient pressure (P5) and temperature (T5) of 2.0 MPa and 864 K, respectively, were used in this experiment. Three identically sized samples were selected in total such that one was preserved as the control sample and the other three were subjected to 150 and 300 shocks, respectively. The supplementary section provides an in-depth description of the shock tube construction and shock wave loading techniques. All of the samples were sent for analytical and functional property measurements, including Raman, HR-TEM, and VSM, once the shock wave loading process was completed. All the analytical instrument details are provided in the supplementary section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acoustic shock-induced transformation of amorphous CQD to graphitic nano structures
ce:section.ce:para: Visible Raman spectroscopy can effectively probe the ordering and disordering signatures in carbon materials and this technique is one of the most effective analytical tools to understand the allotropic structural features of carbon materials [40,41]. Herein, we have adopted a similar Raman spectroscopic technique to understand the impact of shock waves on the amorphous CQD and the recorded Raman spectra of the control and shocked samples are presented in Fig. 1 .

According to the observed Raman spectra of the control sample, it has two characteristic Raman bands at 1377 and 1579 cm−1 and these Raman band locations belong to the respective typical D and G bands of the carbon. Based on the obtained Raman band locations and G-band intensity level, it is clear that the control sample belongs to the amorphous state with mixed sp2 and sp3 hybridization which is found to be well corroborated with the previous reports [42]. Note that, the amorphous carbon particles can exist in a variety of forms such as amorphous carbon with pure sp2 [42], amorphous carbon with pure sp2 and sp3 [42] amorphous carbon with tetrahedral configuration [42], and diamond-like amorphous carbon [42]. For more details about the fingerprints of these amorphous carbon structures, please refer to the recent review article [42]. As appears in the Raman spectra of Fig. 1, under shocked conditions, there are not many changes that could be visible to the naked eye and hence the Lorentzian peak fitting functions were performed for more precise measurements of the Raman peak shift and intensity changes such that the obtained Raman peak profiles are shown in Fig. 2 (a–c). The control and shocked samples are found to be well-fitted with the Lorentzian peak fitting functions thereby from the peak fitting values, subtle changes are found in the intensity and peak shift under shocked conditions. Interestingly, the ID/IG ratio is found to have reduced with respect to the number of shock pulses and the corresponding plot is presented in Fig. 2d. Note that the ID/IG ratio generally represents the degree of graphitization in the test sample and for pure graphitic samples, the ratio must be very low or nearly equal to zero. The observed ID/IG ratio values are 0.78, 0.77 and 0.75 for the control, 150 and 300 shocks, respectively. The reduction of the ID/IG ratio value with respect to the number of shock pulses clearly suggests that, during the shocked conditions, the graphitic particles have been formed which is based on the hot-spot nucleation mechanism [37,43] and similar kinds of results have been found in the amorphous carbon nanotubes [37], amorphous reduced graphene oxide NPs [38] and amorphous carbon nanoparticles [44] under shocked conditions.

As the continuation of the ID/IG ratio profile with respect to the number of shock pulses, in Fig. 3 , the separate peak shift profiles and peak width profiles are presented with respect to the number of shock pulses. The D-band undergoes a considerable shift under shocked conditions i.e. located at 1389 and 1378 cm−1 for the respective 150 and 300 shocked conditions. The G-band shows a downward trend with respect to the number of shock pulses such that the Raman band locations are identified at 1579, 1576, and 1574 cm−1. For a reasonable understanding of the formation of graphitic structures, the G-band peak features are more highly considered compared to the D-band and hence a similar logical interpretation is followed as existing in the literature to explain the obtained results. In Fig. 3b, the G-band shows a linear downtrend with respect to the number of shock pulses which is one of the reasonable indications for the formation of the graphic structures under shocked conditions [40,41]. Followed by the peak shift changes with respect to the number of shock pulses, the D and G bands show significant changes in the values of full width at maximum. For example, the D-band width is linearly increased with respect to the number of shock pulses and in contrast, the G-band represents a downtrend against the number of shock pulses. The increment of the G-band intensity and decrement of the values of full width at half maximum clearly represent that the net graphitization is increased because of the cumulative enhancement of the sp2 hybridized carbons in the test samples. It was found that the degree of graphitization is increased with respect to the number of shock pulses.

From the obtained ID/IG ratio and the G-band shift profile, it is clear that some of the amorphous carbon structures have been converted into graphitic structures under shocked conditions and the reasons behind the formation of graphitic structures are, according to the previous literature reports, that the thermal transport properties such as thermal diffusivity and thermal conductivity values have played a major role in shock wave-induced dynamic re-crystallization as well as hot spot nucleation mechanisms [45,46]. Low thermal transport ability materials undergo a pronouncing dynamic recrystallization process and as a result, significant changes take place in their crystallographic structures [46]. In this aspect, the values of thermal conductivity of the amorphous carbon and graphite are 2.2 [47] and 1–250 W/m.K [48], respectively and based on the obtained values, the amorphous carbon has a sufficiently low thermal conductivity value compared to the graphite. In addition to that, all the carbon allotropy structures are more sensitive to the pure temperature effects than that of the pure pressure effects and hence under shocked conditions, significant changes could take place due to the cumulative transient temperature–pressure effects. Because of the poor thermal conductivity of the amorphous carbon, during the shocked conditions, the transient temperature may not be dissipated very fast in amorphous material such that this slow dissipation of the heat might provide the required latent heat to break the sp3 configuration carbons to turn into the sp2 carbons. In such cases, the non-six-fold hexagonal aromatic rings are converted into the perfect hexagonal aromatic rings thereby increasing the net value of graphitization in the test sample. To verify this concept, similar kinds of experiments have been performed with the low ID/IG ratio (graphite and graphene) and no significant changes in G-band shift and ID/IG ratio have been found under shocked conditions and the observed results are not presented here as it has to be reported into a separate research article. From this observation, it is clear that the properties of low thermal diffusivity and conductivity can play a significant role in dynamic recrystallization during the shocked conditions.

Note that the amorphous sample may have a variety of defects such as bond-angle, bond-bending disorder and ring disorder as well as it may have dangling bonds which have led to the formation of non-hexagonal structures [40,41]. Due to the shock wave-induced dynamic recrystallization, the above-mentioned disordered structures have been considerably reduced and as a result, the formation of the perfect hexagonal structures could be found in the test sample and hence the ID/IG ratio is found to be reduced with respect to the number of shock pulses. The possible schematic diagram of the possible conversion of the non-hexagonal structures to hexagonal structures under shocked conditions is presented in Fig. 4 .

So far, the changes in the ID/IG ratio and degree of graphitization in the amorphous CQD under shocked conditions have been discussed. However, in the present case, the changes in the ID/IG ratio are considerably lower compared to the amorphous carbon tubes [37], reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [38], and amorphous carbon nanoparticles [44] under shocked conditions. The major possible reason is, because of the higher particle size, that the applied shock transient temperature can effectively initiate the dynamic recrystallization, whereas in the case of ultra-small size particles (Ex: quantum dots), the applied thermal energy cannot sustain longer inside the lattice due to the existence of limited lattice region and hence the applied energy is easily dissipated from the lattice. Because of this reason, the applied shock waves could not initiate the pronounced degree of graphitization in quantum dots as that of the conventional carbon nanotubes [37] and particles [44].

The HR-TEM images of the control and shocked CQD and the respective histogram profiles are presented in Fig. 5 . As seen in Fig. 5(a and b), the control CQD has a spherical shape wherein the dot sizes appear ranging from 12 to 24 (Fig. 5c). Under shocked conditions, subtle size changes can be seen whereas no shape change could be witnessed under 150 and 300 shocked conditions. Fig. 5 e and f clearly show that all the dots retain their original shapes under shocked conditions.

Furthermore, to verify the degree of graphitization, magnified atomic fingerprint regions (Moiré fringe) and SEAD patterns are portrayed in Fig. 6 . As seen in Fig. 6(a–c), the absence of the Moiré fringe patterns (Fig. 6b) clearly demonstrates that the control sample belongs to the amorphous state and the obtained SEAD pattern (Fig. 6c) also well supports the fact of the existing amorphous state which is due to the absence of the bright sextets or even diffused ring pattern for the (002) plane. However, at the 150-shocked condition, the intensity of the ring (002) is found to be slightly increased (Fig. 6f).

At the 300-shocked condition, the appearance of the Moiré fringe patterns could be witnessed which belong to the (002) plane (insert of Fig. 6h) and in Fig. 6i–a considerable increment of the SAED pattern could be seen. These factors clearly demonstrate that the graphitic structures have been formed at the 300-shocked condition such that the HR-TEM results are found to be well corroborated with the Raman spectroscopic results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contribution of the amorphous and crystalline graphitic nanostructures in magnetic ordering
ce:section.ce:para: The element “carbon” falls outside the magnetic elements group as its atomic structure brackets it as diamagnetic. Theoretically, allotropies of carbon such as graphite, graphene and diamond belong to the diamagnetic state [49]. However, carrying out experiments to achieve a flawless diamagnetic state in carbon materials poses a considerable challenge due to the presence of defects and topological frustration [50–52]. Due to the appearance of the defects, all the conjugated π electrons cannot be paired simultaneously such that they significantly affect the spin moments in the s and p orbitals which can lead to instability of the π-electron system and hence magnetic order can take place in the defective carbon structures [52]. These defects can make them to susceptible to magnetic fields, and the strength of the magnetic properties is directly related to the defect density [38,50–52]. Following the discovery of magnetic responses in carbon-based materials, the study of magnetism within carbon materials has gained significant attention among carbon researchers. Moreover, due to the observed magnetic behavior in carbon, it is now possible to draw conclusive insights into the impact of shock wave-induced graphitization on amorphous carbon materials. To investigate this phenomenon, a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) was employed to examine the magnetic properties at room temperature. The resulting M − H loops for both the control and shocked samples are depicted in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7, the control sample exhibits a notably high saturation magnetization, and its hysteresis loop categorizes it as possessing a weak room temperature ferromagnetic state which is in alignment with the prior findings [38]. It's important to note that the emergence of a weak ferromagnetic state in the control sample could be attributed to the substantial presence of defects and non-hexagonal structures. Additionally, this phenomenon is evidently linked to the abundance of weak ferromagnetic or paramagnetic spins within the sample. When the defect count is elevated, there is a corresponding increase in saturation magnetization [38]. Under shocked conditions, it is found that the saturation magnetization linearly decreases for the 150 and 300 shocks and the obtained VSM loops are presented in Fig. 7.

In addition to that, rather than the saturation magnetization, another interesting behavior is also found in the control sample VSM loop. Note that the saturation magnetization has a higher value in the low magnetic field which linearly reduces while increasing the field. It is one of the typical signatures for the defects-induced weak room temperature ferromagnetic state materials and similar kinds of VSM loops have been reported for the theoretically diamagnetic materials such as ZnO, CeO2, and TiO2, respectively [53–55]. The role of some kinds of defects [52] (for example in carbon materials: unsaturated dangling bonds) in magnetic ordering is considerably reduced at higher fields and hence they try to become an antimagnetic material such that the saturation magnetization is reduced at higher fields. For better understanding of this concept, the zoomed-in portions of the M − H loops are displayed in Fig. 8 a wherein a straight line is drawn from the starting point to the endpoint for the control and shocked samples. For more convenience, the drawn lines are separately presented for a better visualization for identifying the changes in saturation magnetization with respect to the field strength.

The control sample has high saturation magnetization (0.869 emu/g) and it is worthwhile to note that a higher saturation magnetization is expected in the regions with a greater physical density of non-sp2 hybridizations. This phenomenon is primarily attributable to the presence of a higher concentration of defects within the system such that it is linearly reduced with respect to the field and it is clearly evident that the control amorphous CQD have a high number of unsaturated dangling bonds, whereas under shocked conditions, the saturation magnetization is linearly reduced with respect to the number of shock pulses and the obtained values are 0.757 and 0.71 emu/g for 150 and 300 shocks, respectively. The observed reduction of saturation magnetization under shocked conditions is clearly demonstrated in such a way that the formation of graphitic structures is adequately pronounced [38,43]. On the other hand, the field-dependent saturation magnetization is not significantly alerted (Δ Ms) at 300-shocked condition (Fig. 8b) which may be because of the formation of graphitic nanostructures with perfect hexagonal sp2 hybridizations. Such graphitic nanostructures have significantly reduced the unsaturated dangling bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship of the magnetic ordering and degree of graphitization
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 9 , the ID/IG intensity ratio and the values of saturation magnetization of both the control and shocked samples with respect to the number of shock pulses are presented to gain a deeper insight into the relationship between the degree of graphitization and saturation magnetization. Interestingly, both of these parameters exhibit a nearly identical trend. The profile obtained indicates that the control sample possesses a higher saturation magnetization value because of its elevated concentration of defects (higher ID/IG value). Conversely, the lower saturation magnetization values observed in the shocked samples provide clear evidence of the emergence of graphitic structures which is particularly evident at the 300-shocked condition. The overall data reveal that the 300-shocked sample contains fewer graphitic structures than that of the control and 150-shocked samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of surface chemistry of the a-CQD in electrical conductivity
ce:section.ce:para: The surface chemistry plays a significant role in the net electrical conductivity of the carbon allotropy structures. The defect-free hexagonal sp2 carbon networks provide a higher electrical conductivity than that of the amorphous carbon due to the sp3 carbons. For example, in the case of the diamond like amorphous carbon structures, the four-fold coordinated sp3 site contains no free electrons, because each carbon atom makes proper bonding with their nearest neighbor's carbon atoms, but the three-fold coordinated sp2 site associated with π– π * bonding contains one free electron per ring [56,57]. Due to these delocalized π-electrons, the 2D graphene and graphite have a higher electrical conductivity than that of the amorphous carbon and diamond. In the present case, to ensure the formation of the sp2 carbons at amorphous CQD during the shock wave loaded conditions, we have performed the electrical conductivity measurement by the impedance spectroscopic analysis and the obtained Nyquist plots of the control and shocked samples are presented in Fig. 10 .

As seen in Fig. 10, the electrical resistance of the shocked samples is considerably low compared to the control samples which clearly demonstrate the formation of sp2 hybridized graphitic particles under shocked conditions. Note that, based on the obtained resistance values, while increasing the number of shock pulses, the electrical resistance is found to be linearly reduced.

In the present work, we have systematically investigated the structural stability of the amorphous state carbon quantum dots under acoustic shocked conditions with respect to the number of shock pulses and a few interesting results have been observed in the purview of spectroscopic, microscopic and magnetic properties. Based on the observed results, it could be reflected, from the Raman spectroscopic data, the ID/IG ratio is slightly reduced under shocked conditions such that the observed values are 0.78, 0.77 and 0.75 for the 0, 150 and 300 shocked conditions. The obtained lower ID/IG value at the 300-shocked condition clearly demonstrates the formation of graphitic nanostructures. The appearance of the (002) plane Moiré fringe patterns and the corresponding SAED pattern provide the most crucial supporting evidence for the formation of graphitic nanostructures at the 300-shocked condition. The values of saturation magnetization (Ms) are 0.869, 0.757 and 0.710 emu/g for the respective 0, 150 and 300 shocked conditions. Nyquist plots’ results clearly demonstrate the reduction of the electrical resistance with respect to the number of shock pulses due to the formation of graphitic particles. Following the analytical results, the intrinsic property of the lower saturation magnetization provides resounding proof for the formation of graphitic nanostructures at the 300-shocked condition. Based on the observed results, even though, there is a noticeable transformation from the amorphous carbon to graphitic nanostructures under shocked conditions, the change in degree of graphitization is much lower than that of the previously reported amorphous carbon nanoparticles, multi-wall carbon nanotubes, and reduced graphene oxide nanoparticles. The observed results provide sufficient data to authenticate the higher structural stability of the amorphous carbon quantum dots under shocked conditions. Hence, the title quantum dots are strongly suggested for the applications of device fabrication.
Not applicable.
All the authors agree to participate.
All people involved in or responsible for the research are informed and consent.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Sivakumar Aswathappa and Lidong Dai - conceptualized the project and Roles/Writing - original draft. S. Sahaya Jude Dhas- Visualization, Roles/Writing - original draft. Priyadharshini Matheswaran-sample preparations and characterizations. Raju Suresh Kumar, Vasanthi Thangavel, V. N. Vijayakumar helped the data processing and analysis. All the authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank NSF of China (42072055). This project was supported by the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R142), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
